feed.title: Wasil Zafar — Philosophy
feed.subtitle: Latest philosophy articles from Wasil Zafar

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 9: Advanced Concepts
entry.summary: Advanced Eastern philosophy — sunyata (emptiness), pratityasamutpada (dependent origination), Madhyamaka two truths, non-dual awareness, Eastern virtue ethics, and phenomenological convergences.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 3: Buddhism
entry.summary: Buddhism — the life of Siddhartha Gautama, Four Noble Truths, Noble Eightfold Path, anatta (no-self), the major schools (Theravada, Mahayana, Vajrayana, Zen), and meditation.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 6: Comparative
entry.summary: Comparative Eastern philosophy — how Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism contrast on the self, society, suffering, and practice; and how Chinese culture integrated all three.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 5: Confucianism
entry.summary: Confucianism — Confucius, the Analects, the cardinal virtues (ren, li, xiao, yi, zhi), the project of self-cultivation, social harmony, and Mencius vs Xunzi on human nature.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 7: East-West Dialogue
entry.summary: East-West philosophical dialogue — substance vs process, autonomous self vs relational self, deductive logic vs paradox, theoretical knowledge vs transformative practice.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 2: Foundations
entry.summary: Foundations of Eastern philosophy — the unity of mind and world, impermanence, harmony, non-dual thinking, and the methodological style that distinguishes Asian traditions.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 10: Modern Applications
entry.summary: Modern applications of Eastern philosophy — clinical mindfulness and CBT, leadership, business strategy, the East Asian developmental model, and AI ethics in 2026.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 8: Practical Philosophy
entry.summary: Practical Eastern philosophy — daily meditation, ethical living, emotional mastery, and integrating Buddhist, Taoist, and Confucian practice into a contemporary life.

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 4: Taoism
entry.summary: Taoism — Laozi and the Tao Te Ching, Zhuangzi

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 12: AI & Machines
entry.summary: AI and the philosophy of mind — Turing test, Searle

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 14: Applications
entry.summary: Applied philosophy of mind — neuroethics, criminal responsibility and the brain, mental health and psychiatric categories, brain-computer interfaces, and the moral status of AI.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 5: Consciousness
entry.summary: The Hard Problem of consciousness — qualia, philosophical zombies, the explanatory gap, Global Workspace Theory, and Integrated Information Theory.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 9: Emotions
entry.summary: Philosophy of emotions — James-Lange feeling theory, cognitive theories (Solomon, Nussbaum), basic emotions vs constructionism (Barrett), and emotions as moral knowledge.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 8: Free Will
entry.summary: Free will — hard determinism, libertarian free will, compatibilism (Hume, Frankfurt, Strawson), Frankfurt cases, Libet

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 4: Functionalism
entry.summary: Functionalism in philosophy of mind — mental states defined by their causal-functional roles. Multiple realizability, Putnam, Fodor, machine functionalism, and the Chinese Room critique.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 6: Intentionality
entry.summary: Intentionality — the

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 13: Modern Debates
entry.summary: Modern debates in philosophy of mind — embodied & extended cognition (4E), the panpsychism revival (Goff, Strawson), Integrated Information Theory, predictive processing, and enactivism.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 10: Perception
entry.summary: Philosophy of perception — direct vs indirect realism, the argument from illusion, sense-data theory, adverbialism, representationalism, disjunctivism, and predictive processing.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 7: Personal Identity
entry.summary: Personal identity over time — Locke

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 3: Physicalism
entry.summary: Physicalism in philosophy of mind — behaviorism, identity theory, eliminative materialism, and the famous arguments (Nagel

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 11: Self-Knowledge
entry.summary: Self-knowledge in philosophy of mind — privileged access, introspection, transparency, the constructive nature of self-reports, confabulation, and self-deception.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 10: Applied Political Philosophy
entry.summary: Applied political philosophy — policy design under value pluralism, criminal justice and punishment theory, economic regulation, public administration, and judging institutions by ideal vs comparative standards.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 6: Equality, Rights & Justice
entry.summary: Equality, rights, and justice — kinds of equality (formal, of opportunity, of outcome), distributive justice debates, the structure of rights, human rights theory, and historical injustice.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 7: Political Ideologies
entry.summary: The major political ideologies — classical and modern liberalism, conservatism, socialism, social democracy, libertarianism, anarchism, fascism — mapped against the building blocks of liberty, equality, and authority.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 2: Justice
entry.summary: Justice in political philosophy — Plato

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 3: Liberty & Freedom
entry.summary: Liberty in political philosophy — Isaiah Berlin

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 9: Freedom in Modern Systems
entry.summary: Freedom in modern systems — surveillance capitalism (Zuboff), platform power, algorithmic governance, AI ethics, the political economy of attention, and the structural threats to liberty in 2026.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 8: Modern Political Philosophy
entry.summary: Modern political philosophy — global justice, feminist political theory, postcolonial and decolonial thought, critical theory and the Frankfurt School, and contemporary democratic theory.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 4: Power, Authority & State
entry.summary: Power, authority, and the state — Weber on legitimacy, Hobbes on sovereignty, Marx on class power, Foucault on disciplinary and biopower, Arendt on political action.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 11: Research & Mastery
entry.summary: Research and mastery in political philosophy — methods (analytic, historical, critical, empirical), how to read primary texts, how to write argument, the indispensable canon, and capstone questions for self-test.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 5: Social Contract
entry.summary: The social contract tradition — Hobbes

entry.title: Eastern Philosophy Part 1: Orientation
entry.summary: An accessible orientation to Eastern Philosophy. Discover what makes Eastern thought distinct, its three great traditions (Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism), and the questions that drive it.

entry.title: Existentialism Part 11: Advanced Topics
entry.summary: Closing the existentialism series — absurdism vs nihilism, lived vs measured time, being-toward-death, narrative identity, and existentialism in the age of AI.

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 4: Albert Camus — The Absurd & Revolt
entry.summary: Albert Camus on the Absurd, the Myth of Sisyphus, the three responses to absurdity, the absurd hero, and the strange affirmation that

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 5: Core Existential Themes
entry.summary: The recurring themes of existentialism across thinkers: freedom and responsibility, anxiety and dread, finitude and death, meaning-making, alienation, and authenticity vs inauthenticity.

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 7: Existential Ethics
entry.summary: Existential ethics: morality without God, created values, radical responsibility, the difference between existential and neurotic guilt, Beauvoir

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: An accessible introduction to Existentialism and the Absurd. Explore individual existence, freedom, meaning-making, and the philosophical movement that shaped 20th-century thought.

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 6: Key Thinkers
entry.summary: The wider existential canon: Nietzsche on the death of God and the Übermensch, Heidegger on Dasein and Being-toward-death, Beauvoir

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 2: Søren Kierkegaard — The Birth of Existential Thought
entry.summary: Søren Kierkegaard, the lonely Danish thinker who fathered existentialism. Explore subjectivity, the three stages of existence, despair, the dizziness of freedom, and the leap of faith.

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 9: Literature, Theater & Film
entry.summary: Existentialism and the Absurd in literature, theater, and film: Camus

entry.title: Existentialism Part 10: Modern Applications
entry.summary: Existentialism in the 21st century — career anxiety, social media identity, climate dread, AI existential risk, burnout as inauthenticity, and authentic leadership.

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 8: Existential Psychology
entry.summary: Existential psychology: Yalom

entry.title: Existentialism & the Absurd Part 3: Jean-Paul Sartre — Radical Freedom & Bad Faith
entry.summary: Jean-Paul Sartre on radical freedom, existence precedes essence, bad faith (mauvaise foi), the famous waiter, the look (le regard), and the demanding ideal of authenticity.

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 2: Dualism
entry.summary: Substance dualism from Plato to Descartes — the cogito, the pineal gland, the interaction problem, property dualism, and Chalmers

entry.title: Philosophy of Mind Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: An introduction to Philosophy of Mind. Explore the core questions about consciousness, the mind-body problem, the self, and whether machines can think.

entry.title: Political Philosophy Part 1: Foundations
entry.summary: An accessible introduction to Political Philosophy. Explore the central questions about justice, liberty, power, authority, and the legitimacy of the state.

entry.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 6: Applied Ethics & Case Studies
entry.summary: Apply ethical principles to real-world moral dilemmas. Explore bioethics, environmental ethics, business ethics, AI ethics, and work through contemporary case studies that challenge our moral reasoning. Applied ethics takes the normative theories we've studied and brings them to bear on specific

entry.summary: Customize Settings Cookie Preferences Essential Cookies (Required) Necessary for the website to function properly. entry.title: Ethics & Moral Philosophy Series Part 5: Moral Relativism & Nihilism
entry.summary: Explore moral relativism, cultural relativism, and moral nihilism. Examine the profound challenges to objective morality and whether ethics can truly be universal or is inevitably culturally determined. Moral relativism is the view that moral judgments are not objectively true or false, but are
